summary: Adversarial spike: can TRUE per-region/per-glyph contrast correction be done at all (any terminal, overlay, compositor, shader, or novel trick), or is it fundamentally impossible? Pressure-test the "impossible" verdict.
logs: Spiking 5 attack vectors on the per-region ideal (Opus)...

ruling.best_path: A native always-on-top click-through NSWindow overlay driven by iTerm2's ScreenStreamer (cell positions) + existing window bounds (geometry) + existing contrast math, painting localized subtitle-style scrims behind at-risk text runs. This is the ONLY vector that actually closes both blockers the first pass called fatal — and it does so by occluding the backdrop locally rather than observing it, so it is technically a "different terminal surface" (an app-owned overlay layer), not iTerm2's own response levers.
ruling.reasoning:
The "impossible" verdict holds for the literal ideal as stated (per-glyph foreground correction against the real see-through backdrop), but it does NOT hold absolutely — it holds for every path that tries to reach the ideal THROUGH a terminal's own color/transparency levers. The decisive distinction the code confirms is between OBSERVABILITY and ACTUATION.

ACTUATION is the binding wall. iterm_api.py:119-135 is the whole truth: every lever term-chameleon can pull (set_foreground_color, set_transparency, set_blur, set_minimum_contrast) is a single session-wide value. osc.py confirms the cross-terminal path is the same (OSC 10/11/4 — session-wide). So even with perfect per-cell backdrop knowledge you can only choose ONE global palette. This kills four of five vectors as routes to the true ideal:

1) SCK exclude-terminal capture (research-grade): genuinely upgrades OBSERVABILITY — it recovers the true pre-composite backdrop instead of the polluted composite that screenshot.py:28 (`screencapture -x`) currently grabs. But it feeds the same global selector in adapt.py. Net effect: a better-informed global, not per-region. Real, low-cost, shippable in isolation — but not the ideal.

2) Minimum Contrast (dead-end as the answer): source-confirmed per-cell, but anchored to the OPAQUE theme bg, structurally blind to the composited backdrop. iTerm2 holds no data representation of the see-through pixels anywhere in the text-color path. No setting fixes the anchor. Useful as a global floor (already wired at iterm_api.py:134), not a solution.

3) Dynamic background-image scrim (research-grade, fails ideal): set_background_image_location IS a live setter on the same object, but set_blend is a single scalar, the alpha bug (#8359) fills transparent pixels with the FOREGROUND color (so no see-through holes), and any region opaque enough to guarantee contrast destroys the glass. Spatially-varying tint, not guaranteed per-glyph contrast.

4) Switch terminal (dead-end): the macOS WindowServer owns the vibrancy/blur backdrop and composites the app OVER it; no app — Ghostty GLSL included (iChannel0 is the terminal's own framebuffer, and enabling a shader disables transparency per #4835) — ever receives backdrop pixels. This is an OS-level impossibility, not an iTerm2 limitation. Leaving iTerm2 gains nothing.

5) Overlay (research-grade — the ONE real path): it sidesteps the actuation wall entirely by NOT using any terminal color lever. It composites an app-owned layer at pixel-exact cell rects. The scrim's contrast floor is independent of the backdrop, so it also sidesteps the observability wall — you stop needing to see the wallpaper, you occlude it. Cell positions come from iTerm2 ScreenStreamer (no OCR), geometry from the AppleScript bounds the repo already ships (iterm_window.py). The existing contrast math (text_contrast.py / pixel_contrast.py) decides which runs need a scrim. The mechanism is real. What demotes it from shippable to research-grade is not impossibility — it is (a) the aesthetic self-contradiction: at real text density the scrims tile into a near-opaque panel, erasing the glass the product exists to sell; and (b) sync fragility: no frame-synced handshake between iTerm's GPU repaint and a separate NSWindow, so scrolling ghosts. iterm_window.py already raising on multi-monitor/negative-origin confirms the geometry edge cases are real today.

So: per-region is genuinely impossible via any terminal's response levers (the literal ideal), and achievable ONLY by abandoning those levers for an app-owned overlay surface — which is why this maps to "only-on-other-terminal" (the overlay is effectively a different rendering surface), with the honest caveat that even there it is research-grade and aesthetically self-defeating as an always-on default.
ruling.if_pursued_cost: Two tiers. CHEAP/SHIPPABLE NOW (do this regardless): swap screenshot.py's `screencapture -x` composite grab for an SCK exclude-terminal capture so the global classifier in adapt.py samples the TRUE backdrop and weights luminance by where text actually sits. ~1 dependency (pyobjc-framework-ScreenCaptureKit), ~100-200 lines, reuses the Screen Recording permission already required. Caveat: on macOS Tahoe (26.x) a plain pip CLI hits TCC error -3801 (non-bundled executables vanish from the Screen Recording UI), so this realistically forces shipping a signed/notarized .app bundle — a meaningful packaging cost that ALSO already threatens the current screencapture approach, so it is worth confronting either way. EXPENSIVE/RESEARCH-GRADE (the only true per-region path): the overlay is a category change from pure-Python osascript+iTerm2-API CLI to a native macOS windowed agent — AppKit NSWindow (Swift or PyObjC), an AXObserver reconcile loop for window geometry, an async ScreenStreamer consumer, a render loop fighting scroll/resize/Spaces, plus Accessibility + Screen Recording permissions and signing/notarization. Realistically weeks, macOS+iTerm2-only, with ongoing breakage on every iTerm/macOS update. Scope any spike narrowly: scrim ONLY hard-fail runs (errors, prompts, selected text), accept scroll lag, draw nothing during fast scroll — and treat it as opt-in experimental "spotlight" mode, never the default, because a full always-on scrim erases the glass the product sells.

vectors.vector: Observability blocker: capture the see-through backdrop (wallpaper + windows behind the terminal) SEPARATELY from the composited result, via ScreenCaptureKit with an exclude-my-terminal SCContentFilter.
vectors.how_it_would_work: SCK exclude-terminal capture gives true per-region backdrop knowledge; blocked downstream by lack of per-cell actuation in iTerm2.
blockers: ACTUATION GAP (the real wall): knowing the backdrop per-cell does not give you per-cell correction. iTerm2's response levers (palette, transparency, blur, minimum_contrast) are all session-wide. You can now compute the ideal per-glyph foreground, but you still cannot APPLY a different color to each cell — you can only choose one global palette. Per-region observability does not unlock per-region actuation; it only lets you pick a better-informed GLOBAL worst-case (e.g. correct against the brightest region under the text, or weight by where text actually is). That is a real improvement over a blind global average, but it is not the true per-glyph ideal.

BLUR + GAMMA MODELING: the backdrop image is the SHARP scene. iTerm2 then applies a Gaussian-ish blur and alpha blend over it before text lands on top. To predict the actual color a glyph sits on, you must model blur radius + blend gamma + transparency. SCK gives you the pre-blur backdrop, not the post-blur composite under the glass. This is tractable (convolve with an estimated kernel, blend at the configured alpha) but it is approximate and needs calibration per terminal/OS — research-grade, not turnkey.

macOS TAHOE (26.x) TCC/BUNDLING REGRESSION: non-bundled executables (a pip-installed Python CLI is exactly this) no longer appear in the Screen Recording privacy UI; ScreenCaptureKit returns TCC error -3801 and capture yields desktop-only. This already affects term-chameleon's current screencapture-CLI approach. Shipping SCK-based capture in a plain pip CLI on Tahoe likely requires distributing a signed .app bundle, which is a meaningful packaging/notarization burden.

MOVING BACKDROP / STALENESS: video behind the glass, scrolling pages, window drags change the backdrop continuously. A periodic still capture is correct only between samples; chasing it per-frame is a different cost class and would still hit the actuation wall.
MULTI-PARTY CAPTURE EXCLUSION: exceptingWindows/excludingApplications handles the terminal cleanly, but overlapping translucent windows above the terminal complicate what 'behind' means.
vectors.cost: Observation piece: LOW cost and genuinely shippable in isolation — one new dependency (pyobjc-framework-ScreenCaptureKit), ~100-200 lines of callback-style pyobjc, reuses Screen Recording permission the project already requires. Full per-region ideal: HEAVY — requires blur/gamma calibration model, a signed/notarized .app bundle for Tahoe, and most importantly an actuation path that does not exist in iTerm2. Platform reach is macOS-only (SCK is Apple-only); the OSC/Kitty/Ghostty/Alacritty path gains nothing here.
vectors.shippable_for_a_niche_tool: Partially, and this is the honest nuance. As a clean terminal utility, the SHIPPABLE win is: replace the current composited screencapture grab with an SCK exclude-terminal capture, so the watcher samples the TRUE backdrop (no terminal text/colors polluting the luminance estimate) and weights the estimate by the region where text actually sits. That is a real, low-complexity quality upgrade to the existing global-preset selector and fits the tool cleanly. The FULL per-glyph ideal does NOT fit a simple utility: it dies on the actuation wall (iTerm2 has no per-cell color API) regardless of how perfectly you observe the backdrop. So: observability is shippable and worth doing; true per-region CORRECTION remains blocked downstream and should not be promised. Recommend shipping the better-informed global correction and dropping the per-glyph framing.

vectors.vector: Per-region readability via a separate transparent always-on-top overlay window (borderless click-through NSWindow) aligned over the iTerm2 window, painting localized subtitle-style scrims/halos behind text where contrast is at risk.
vectors.how_it_would_work:
The overlay sidesteps BOTH blockers the first pass declared fatal, and that is the genuinely new result here.

Blocker 1 (no per-cell response lever): An overlay does not need one. It is a second app-owned surface that composites ON TOP of (or just behind) the glyphs at exact pixel rectangles, so it provides true per-region control that iTerm2's session-wide palette/transparency/blur/minimum_contrast can never give. Mechanism: a borderless NSWindow, styleMask .borderless, backgroundColor .clear, isOpaque=false, ignoresMouseEvents=true (confirmed click-through, ignoresMouseEvents | Apple docs), window level above the terminal (e.g. .floating / CGWindowLevel), collectionBehavior set to follow spaces. Its content view draws per-region scrims: a semi-opaque rounded rect or radial halo under each at-risk text run. Standard subtitle/letterbox technique — a ~50-70% opaque box guarantees a contrast floor REGARDLESS of the backdrop (Ian Hamilton / Matinee subtitle guidance), which is exactly why it defeats the 'backdrop unobservable' problem: you stop needing to observe the backdrop at all. You are not measuring the wallpaper; you are occluding it locally.

Blocker 2 (backdrop unobservable / WHERE is the text): solved WITHOUT screen-capture or OCR. iTerm2's own Python API streams cell-accurate content: session.get_screen_streamer() pushes a ScreenContents on every screen change; LineContents.string_at(x) + the style flag give which cells are non-blank and their fg/bg (iterm2.com/python-api/screen.html). session.grid_size gives rows x cols (iterm2.com/python-api/session.html). Window origin/size in points comes from the AppleScript bounds path the repo ALREADY ships (src/term_chameleon/iterm_window.py). Cell pixel size = window content size / grid_size. So you can compute the exact rectangle of every glyph run analytically — no vision pipeline, no OCR, no per-pixel backdrop guess. The repo already has the luminance/contrast math (text_contrast.py, pixel_contrast.py, contrast.py) to decide WHICH runs need a scrim and how opaque, optionally fed by one cheap ScreenCaptureKit/CGWindowList sample of the region behind each run to make scrims minimal (only as dark as needed).

So the data path is: ScreenStreamer (cells+styles, event-driven) -> compute per-run rects from grid+bounds -> decide scrim opacity from contrast target -> overlay redraws only changed regions. The scrim's contrast guarantee is independent of blur/gamma modeling, which is what made the see-through approach impossible before.

This genuinely opens the per-region ideal in principle. The reason it is research-grade rather than shippable is sync robustness and the aesthetic contradiction below.
blockers: SYNC / TRACKING FRAGILITY (the core engineering risk): the overlay must stay pixel-aligned with terminal content through scroll, type, resize, window move, Mission Control, and Spaces. iTerm2 ScreenStreamer fires on content change but there is no frame-synced handshake between iTerm's GPU repaint and a separate process's NSWindow redraw, so during fast scroll the scrim lags the text by 1+ frames and visibly smears/ghosts. Window move/resize via AXObserver kAXWindowMovedNotification is event-driven but not frame-synced either (alt-tab-macos notes observers need a reconcile loop), so the scrim trails the moving window. macOS Sonoma+ also degraded window-capture framerate (obs-studio #10636), worsening any capture-assisted variant.

AESTHETIC SELF-CONTRADICTION: the product's entire value prop is 'preserve the translucent glass feel.' A subtitle scrim that is opaque enough to guarantee contrast is, by definition, an opaque patch over the glass — under every line of text. At realistic text density (a full terminal screen) the scrims tile into a near-solid panel, which is just an opaque terminal with extra steps. The scrim only stays subtle on sparse text; on dense output it erases the glass the tool exists to protect.

PERMISSIONS + DISTRIBUTION: needs Accessibility (AX bounds/observer) AND, for any backdrop sampling, Screen Recording; plus iTerm2's Python API (already a dependency). That is a heavyweight permission stack for a pip-installed CLI, and AX requires a non-sandboxed signed app. Today the repo is pure-Python over osascript + the iTerm2 API; an overlay forces a native AppKit/Swift (or PyObjC) GUI component and a long-lived windowed process — a category change from 'CLI utility' to 'menu-bar app.'

GEOMETRY EDGE CASES the repo already flags: multi-monitor and negative-origin windows break the points->pixels mapping (iterm_window.py raises on left!=0/top!=0), fullscreen disables grid_size, and font ligatures/wide CJK/Powerline glyphs make 'cell = width/cols' an approximation that drifts the scrim off the glyph. Cursor blink and reflow on resize add churn.

POSITION RELATIVE TO GLYPH: a scrim BEHIND the glyph requires the overlay below iTerm but above the desktop/other apps — impossible, because the see-through backdrop is whatever app is actually behind iTerm, not a layer you can insert into. So the scrim must go ON TOP of the glyph and the glyph must be re-rendered over it, OR the scrim sits on top at low opacity as a 'tint,' which dims the user's own text. There is no clean z-order that puts an app-owned layer between iTerm's text and iTerm's transparent background.
vectors.cost: High / category-changing. Moves the project from a pure-Python osascript+iTerm2-API CLI to a native macOS windowed agent: AppKit NSWindow overlay (Swift or PyObjC), an AXObserver reconcile loop for window geometry, an async iTerm2 ScreenStreamer consumer, per-region contrast decisioning (reuses existing color math), and optional ScreenCaptureKit sampling to minimize scrim darkness. Plus Accessibility + Screen Recording permissions, code signing/notarization for AX, and a real render loop fighting scroll/resize. Realistically weeks of work and an ongoing maintenance surface (every iTerm/macOS update can shift bounds, levels, capture behavior). Platform reach: macOS+iTerm2 only — the OSC cross-terminal story (Kitty/Ghostty/Alacritty) gets nothing from this, since the cell-stream + bounds APIs are iTerm-specific.
vectors.shippable_for_a_niche_tool: No, not as the product's main mode — but it is the one vector that genuinely achieves the per-region ideal, so it earns 'research-grade' rather than 'dead-end.' The mechanism is real and provably defeats the two blockers the first pass called fatal (the scrim's contrast guarantee removes the need to observe the backdrop; the iTerm2 ScreenStreamer + grid_size + existing window bounds give cell-exact positions without OCR). What kills it as a shippable default is not impossibility — it is (1) the aesthetic self-contradiction (dense text = the scrims become an opaque panel, erasing the glass the tool sells) and (2) sync fragility (no frame-synced handshake between iTerm's GPU repaint and a separate NSWindow, so scrolling ghosts). For a clean, simple terminal utility, the honest call is: keep the global worst-case preset path as the shipped default, and treat the overlay as an opt-in experimental 'spotlight' mode — scrim ONLY the few runs that fail contrast hard (errors, prompts, selected text), accept slight scroll lag, and let it degrade by simply not drawing during fast scroll. That narrow framing is plausibly shippable later; a full per-glyph always-on scrim is not, for this product's identity.

vectors.vector: Leave iTerm2 for a terminal with per-cell/per-region background or GPU shader control (Kitty, Ghostty, WezTerm, Alacritty) to make true per-region readability correction native.
vectors.how_it_would_work:
The ideal needs two things at once: (a) a response lever with per-region granularity, and (b) knowledge of the actual backdrop pixels showing through EACH region. The platform survey shows the granularity lever exists in places, but the backdrop input does not exist anywhere, which is the binding constraint.

GRANULARITY LEVERS THAT DO EXIST (none of which solve the problem):
- Ghostty: real GLSL custom-shader pipeline (ShaderToy format, custom-shader=, stackable). The shader runs as a POST-PROCESS pass whose only input texture is iChannel0 = Ghostty's own rendered terminal framebuffer, plus iResolution/iTime uniforms. It can do per-pixel contrast math on the terminal's own glyphs, but it has zero access to the desktop behind the window. Worse, enabling a shader breaks transparency (discussion #4835): the shader outputs opaque pixels over the layer background, so the see-through effect is lost. So even the one terminal with true per-pixel GPU control cannot see the backdrop AND turning the shader on removes the very glass you are trying to correct against.
- Kitty: dynamic_background_opacity + a list of up to 7 background colors each with its own opacity (per-cell-color transparency), and per-window background images via remote control / set-background-image. This is genuinely per-region-ish control of how transparent each cell is, but it still has no backdrop pixels to base a decision on, and Kitty deliberately has NO user GLSL shaders (Kovid: would need a meta-shading language to target GLSL/SPIR-V/Metal; rejected — issue #8250, discussion #6651). CRTty injects shaders via LD_PRELOAD but that is an unsupported hack and still only sees the framebuffer.
- WezTerm: config.background supports stacked layers (color/gradient/image) with per-layer opacity and inactive_pane_hsb dimming, all driven from Lua. But layers are window-level, sourced from files/gradients, with no documented runtime per-frame Lua update of layer content and no backdrop sampling. WebGpu front_end additionally breaks window_background_opacity on macOS (issues #2952, #6359, #3773), so the GPU path is currently broken for the transparency case anyway.
- Alacritty: no background image, no shaders, only window opacity. Strictly less capable.

THE BACKDROP IS UNOBSERVABLE AT THE OS LEVEL, NOT THE TERMINAL LEVEL. Confirmed render order on macOS: for a translucent region the WINDOW SERVER renders the vibrancy/glass backdrop (a blurred, tinted version of everything behind that region) and composites the application's framebuffer OVER it. Behind-window blur is only obtainable via NSVisualEffectView/NSGlassEffectView, which talk privately to the window server; the app supplies a vibrant region and gets a visual result on screen but never receives the backdrop pixels as a readable texture. This is by design (security/performance). So no terminal — present or hypothetical, GLSL or not — gets the per-region backdrop, because the compositor owns it and hands the app nothing. A GPU shader that "samples the backdrop and boosts local contrast" is impossible: the backdrop simply is not in the shader's address space; it is added by a later compositor stage the app cannot read.

WHY screencapture cannot close the gap either: capturing the screen returns the ALREADY-composited pixels (your terminal blended over the backdrop), governed by ScreenCaptureKit / CGWindowList; you cannot isolate the pre-composite backdrop, and the blur+gamma the window server applied are not modeled. So you cannot feed a per-region backdrop into a shader from a capture loop. The only correction signal you can derive from capture is a coarse, laggy estimate of overall brightness — which is exactly the global classifier term-chameleon already ships.
blockers: OS-level, not iTerm2-level: the macOS window server renders the see-through/blur backdrop and composites the app over it; the app (any terminal) never receives backdrop pixels as a readable texture. NSVisualEffectView/NSGlassEffectView give a visual result, not data.

Ghostty's GLSL shader — the closest thing to native per-pixel control — only receives iChannel0 = the terminal's own framebuffer, never the backdrop; and enabling a shader disables window transparency (discussion #4835), destroying the glass effect itself.
Kitty has per-cell-color opacity but no user shaders (deliberately rejected, issue #8250) and still no backdrop input.
WezTerm background layers are window-level, file/gradient-sourced, no per-frame runtime update, no backdrop sampling; WebGpu breaks opacity on macOS (issues #2952/#6359).

screencapture/ScreenCaptureKit returns post-composite pixels; the pre-blur backdrop is unrecoverable and the window-server blur+gamma are unmodeled, so a capture loop cannot supply per-region backdrop to any shader.

Even with a perfect backdrop signal, none of the four terminals exposes a real-time, per-cell FOREGROUND-color lever keyed to per-cell backdrop — only Ghostty could do per-pixel math, and only on its own framebuffer with transparency off.
vectors.cost: Switching terminals abandons the entire macOS+iTerm2 scope (iTerm2 Python API, profiles, the watch daemon) for at best a lateral move that still cannot reach the ideal. Building a Ghostty-shader path would be research-grade GLSL work that produces a contrast-boost over the terminal's OWN colors with transparency turned off — i.e., not glass correction at all. High complexity, zero payoff toward the stated ideal.
vectors.shippable_for_a_niche_tool: No. For a clean, simple terminal utility this vector adds a second platform, GLSL/Lua maintenance, and broken-transparency edge cases while still failing the ideal. The honest, shippable design remains what term-chameleon already does: observe a coarse global backdrop-brightness signal and adapt session-wide foreground+transparency with hysteresis (global worst-case correction). True per-region/per-glyph correction is not achievable on any of these terminals because the backdrop is owned by the OS compositor and never exposed to the application; leaving iTerm2 does not change that. Recommend documenting per-region as an OS-level impossibility and keeping the global adaptive approach as the intended, correct design — not a fallback.

vectors.vector: iTerm2 Minimum Contrast as a possible already-existing per-cell solution: does it composite against the real see-through backdrop at render time, or only against the opaque theme/cell background color?
vectors.how_it_would_work:
Minimum Contrast IS genuinely per-cell, but it composites against the wrong background — the opaque theme/cell color, never the see-through backdrop. Proven from primary source (gnachman/iTerm2):

RENDER PATH (sources/MetalRenderer/Glue/iTermMetalPerFrameState.m, ~L2446): for each character iTerm2 calls
  [colorMap processedTextColorForTextColor:rawColor overBackgroundColor:ColorForVector(unprocessedBackgroundColor) disableMinimumContrast:...]
so contrast adjustment IS evaluated per glyph against that glyph's own background. Good — the per-cell machinery the ideal wants already exists.

WHAT THE BACKGROUND ACTUALLY IS (same file, backgroundColorForCharacter: L315-331): unprocessedBackgroundColor is built ONLY from the screen_char_t fields c.backgroundColor/bgGreen/bgBlue/bgColorMode (the cell's nominal palette/RGB), resolved through the color map and fastProcessedBackgroundColorForBackgroundColor:. There is zero sampling of transparency, blur, background image, or anything behind the window.

THE CONTRAST MATH (sources/ExternalSessionState/iTermColorMap.m, processedTextColorForTextColor: L278-296): it pulls backgroundRgb = [backgroundColor getComponents] and calls
  [NSColor getComponents:contrastingRgb forComponents:textRgb withContrastAgainstComponents:backgroundRgb minimumContrast:_minimumContrast]
i.e. it forces a perceived-brightness delta of textRgb against the OPAQUE cell-bg RGB triple. minimumContrast (set session-wide by term-chameleon at iterm_api.py:134 via set_minimum_contrast) only scales how aggressively text is pushed toward black/white relative to THAT triple.

WHERE TRANSPARENCY ENTERS (too late, and only as alpha): transparency/blur/background-image are applied downstream as (a) the .w/alpha channel on the bg color and (b) a window-level top-view alpha (processedDefaultBackgroundColor L822-838, iTermAlphaValueForTopView using _transparencyAlpha and _backgroundImageBlend). The RGB used for the contrast brightness comparison is the fully opaque color; alpha is appended afterward and is never fed back into the contrast computation. The real see-through pixels are produced by the macOS WindowServer compositing the semi-transparent layer over whatever is behind it — those composited pixels never exist as data inside iTerm2 and are never read back.

NET: iTerm2 already does per-cell contrast correction, but against the theme background, exactly as the first pass claimed. Confirmed against source, not just docs. The docs corroborate ('guarantee a minimum level of brightness difference between the foreground and background color of every character'; 'Minimum contrast never modifies background colors').
blockers: The contrast comparison uses the opaque cell/theme background RGB (screen_char_t -> colorMap), NOT the composited translucent backdrop. Turning minimum_contrast up only fights the theme bg harder; it does nothing about the wallpaper/windows showing through.

Transparency, blur, and background-image enter only as an alpha/top-view-alpha at a LATER GPU compositing stage and are never fed back into the brightness-difference calculation. So even at minimum_contrast=100 (all text forced pure black/white vs theme bg) the actual readable contrast against the see-through backdrop is uncontrolled.

The see-through pixels are composited by the macOS WindowServer, not by iTerm2. iTerm2 has no data representation of the backdrop at any point in the text-color path, so there is nothing per-cell to correct against — the per-cell hook exists but is structurally blind to the real background.
minimum_contrast is also session-wide (single _minimumContrast scalar), so there is no API to vary the target per region even if the data existed.
vectors.cost: Zero additional cost to confirm — the lever is already wired (iterm_api.py set_minimum_contrast). But it cannot deliver the ideal at any setting because it is mathematically anchored to the wrong (opaque, theme) background. Note one real downside flagged in docs: enabling minimum contrast forces a more complex GPU blend and disables GPU rendering when combined with transparent windows — exactly term-chameleon's use case — so cranking it has a performance cost with no correctness payoff for the backdrop problem.
vectors.shippable_for_a_niche_tool: Yes to ship minimum_contrast as a global readability floor (it usefully guarantees text-vs-theme-bg legibility and the tool already sets it) — but NO as the per-region/per-glyph-vs-backdrop solution. This attack vector does not collapse the problem. It confirms the first pass: per-cell correction against the actual see-through backdrop is not achievable through Minimum Contrast, because the feature is per-cell yet anchored to the opaque theme background and is structurally blind to the composited backdrop. The global worst-case design stands; minimum_contrast is a complementary floor, not the answer.
sources: https://github.com/gnachman/iTerm2 sources/MetalRenderer/Glue/iTermMetalPerFrameState.m (backgroundColorForCharacter: L315-331; processedTextColor call L2446-2447; processedDefaultBackgroundColor + iTermAlphaValueForTopView L822-838; transparencyAlpha L506)
https://iterm2.com/documentation-preferences-profiles-colors.html (Minimum Contrast: guarantees brightness difference between fg and bg of every character; never modifies background colors)
https://iterm2.com/faq.html / iterm2.com docs (minimum contrast disables GPU rendering with transparent windows, performance penalty)

vectors.vector: Dynamic per-region background-image scrim pushed live to iTerm2 (set_background_image_location + blend), computed as a "negative"/scrim of a ScreenCaptureKit backdrop capture, with max-radius blur as a homogenizer fallback. Tested against the three sub-mechanisms in the brief.
vectors.how_it_would_work: The lever is real: LocalWriteOnlyProfile.set_background_image_location(path), set_background_image_mode, and set_blend(0..1) ARE runtime session-local setters on the exact same object term-chameleon already drives in live_iterm.py (session.async_set_profile_properties(change)). Adding ('set_background_image_location', path) to iterm_api.setter_mappings is mechanically trivial. The image is painted behind text, and per iTerm2 issue #627 a background image with transparency on composites WITH the see-through layer ('a mix between my desktop background, desktop icons, other applications and iTerm background') -- so a darkening scrim image CAN tint what shows through; vector (1) is not categorically impossible. Intended math: ScreenCaptureKit captures the full display, mask out the terminal own-window rect (via CGWindowID bounds) to recover the backdrop showing through, downsample to the cell grid, compute a per-cell scrim pushing each region toward a target luminance so glyph fg keeps >=4.5:1, render that scrim PNG, write it, set_background_image_location to it. Max blur (radius cap 30) is the homogenizer for vector (3).
blockers: BLEND IS A SINGLE SCALAR, NOT PER-PIXEL: set_blend is one 0..1 value for the whole image blended against the background COLOR uniformly. The image is not composited per-pixel against the live wallpaper with controllable local opacity. You can bake spatial variation into the PNG itself, but its strength over the see-through layer is governed globally, so you cannot independently dial 'opaque scrim here, transparent there' relative to the wallpaper.

ALPHA IS BROKEN (issue #8359): transparent PNG pixels are filled with the profile FOREGROUND color, not left see-through and not the background color. So a scrim PNG that is transparent where the backdrop is already safe does NOT preserve the see-through aesthetic there -- it paints solid foreground. This defeats vector (2) (using the image as a 'negative' to flatten effective background): the negative cannot have see-through holes.

OCCLUSION TRADE-OFF KILLS THE AESTHETIC: to genuinely flatten a bright region you must make the scrim opaque there, which removes the very translucency the tool exists to preserve. Per-region correction strong enough to fix a hard region is per-region destruction of see-through. There is no middle setting that both keeps see-through AND guarantees contrast on an arbitrary bright patch.

NO CLOSED FEEDBACK LOOP: the realized on-screen result = wallpaper (moving/arbitrary) -> window transparency -> iTerm Gaussian blur (unmodeled) -> blend with scrim+bg color -> gamma -> text. screencapture/ScreenCaptureKit of the terminal sees this COMPOSITED result, and capturing the backdrop requires masking the terminal rect and assumes the backdrop is static between capture and render. A moving video wallpaper or scrolling window behind it desyncs immediately. You are computing a scrim for a backdrop that no longer exists by the time it paints.

BLUR DOES NOT FULLY HOMOGENIZE: iTerm2 blur radius caps at 30 and is documented (#2960) as weaker than macOS Terminal. A Gaussian at r=30 smears but does not collapse a high-contrast wallpaper (white window edge next to black dock) to a single flat color across a full terminal width; low-frequency luminance structure survives, so per-region variance remains. Blur-as-homogenizer only 'suffices' for already-low-frequency backdrops, which are exactly the easy case the global worst-case design already handles.

BACKGROUND IMAGE IS PROFILE/SESSION SCOPED, NOT CELL SCOPED: confirms the first pass -- there is still no per-cell control surface anywhere in the iTerm2 API. The image is one texture for the whole session.
vectors.cost: High and ongoing. Net-new deps: ScreenCaptureKit (Swift/PyObjC bridge or a helper binary) plus Screen Recording permission prompt (UX friction, MDM blockers). A live render pipeline: capture -> mask terminal rect -> per-cell luminance -> scrim synthesis -> PNG encode -> set_background_image_location, every frame the backdrop moves, fighting blur/gamma you cannot observe. New PNG writer beyond the current PPM-only images.py. Versus the current architecture: term-chameleon today is a clean session-wide color/transparency/blur adjuster with PPM-based offline contrast estimation -- this vector roughly triples the moving parts and adds a permission-gated native capture dependency for a result that is still only approximate.
vectors.shippable_for_a_niche_tool: No -- not as TRUE per-region correction. It does not fit a clean simple terminal utility and it does not actually deliver the ideal: the single-scalar blend, foreground-fill alpha bug, occlusion-vs-see-through trade-off, and absent feedback loop mean you get a spatially-varying TINT, not guaranteed per-glyph contrast against the real backdrop. The honest shippable distillation of this research IS the global worst-case design the first pass landed on, optionally enriched two ways that stay simple: (a) a STATIC pre-baked subtle scrim image (vignette/gradient) shipped as a profile asset to lift the global floor without any runtime capture -- pure aesthetic, no per-region claim; (b) keep the existing transparency/blur/minimum_contrast levers and, if anything, raise the blur as a soft homogenizer with an honest 'works best on low-frequency wallpapers' caveat. True per-region remains research-grade: physically gestured-at by set_background_image_location but blocked by per-pixel blend absence, the alpha bug, the see-through/occlusion conflict, and the unobservable moving backdrop. Verdict: research-grade, lean global for the actual product.
sources: src/term_chameleon/iterm_api.py (setter_mappings -- where set_background_image_location would slot in)
src/term_chameleon/pixel_contrast.py and images.py (current PPM-only, offline percentile-cluster contrast estimate)
iTerm2 Python API profile.py: set_background_image_location / set_background_image_mode / set_blend / set_transparency / set_blur / set_blur_radius docstrings
iTerm2 GitLab issue #627 -- background image composites WITH see-through wallpaper, not instead of it
iTerm2 GitLab issue #8359 -- transparent background-image pixels filled with foreground color (alpha broken)
iTerm2 GitLab issue #2960 -- blur radius weaker/capped vs macOS Terminal
Apple ScreenCaptureKit docs -- display capture + per-window inclusion; no native exclude-one-window, must mask terminal rect to recover backdrop
iTerm2 blending.html Python API example -- async_set_blend is a single 0..1 scalar
